content:
I am reviewing the THIS_MODULE series sent by @Kari Argillander and I think I understand why generics is needed here.

The problem is that a constant is needed for module pointer, and whether this is &__this_module or NULL depends on whether the crate is built-in or compiled as a module.

As we build VTABLE using generics, this needs to be something exposed via the type system, and cannot just be a reference to THIS_MODULE (which is a static that we generate per crate).

However, I think the approach in the series is quite verbose and thus non-ideal. I think there're two simpler approaches:

  1. Have #[vtable] macro implicitly add a reference to THIS_MODULE (Just the constant, so no trait is needed)
  2. Change the C side so that instead of NULL, we have a __this_module for built-in modules. Then we can unconditionally reference __this_module symbol, and it will resolve to either the special built-in __this_module, or the specific one for removable modules.

I was also thinking that in future we will make it so that vtable macro will automatically add THIS_MODULE so users does not need to add those themselves. I was still thinking this series was good starting point. At least hopefully it gives ideas because currently I think module handling is not ideal state.

I have to say that I do not quite yet understand your proposal. I have to check how C side is implemented.
I failed to see why trait is needed in your series? Without C side changes and #[vtable] changes, why cannot the module just provide a const that gives you the pointer to the required THIS_MODULE?
I wanted to separate that Kernel crate cannot use THIS_MODULE. So Kernel crate can only consume it and modules can give it but not basically use it.
I am not getting your point. If you use a const in the trait the kernel crate cannot use it either unless supplied by a trait impl.
I'm not defending my approx. That is how it turns out after 4 iteration. Of course there could be better approx. I'm happy to hear other ways to improve it.
